
<br> 
<center><img src="https://i.imgur.com/AbCCpQO.png" width="700"></center>


_____

<a id='TOC'></a>

# Spatial data 

_____

1. [Simple Map](#part1)

2. [Choropleths](#part2)

3. [Merging](#part3) 

4. [Creating spatial points](#part4) 




_____

```{r klippy, echo=FALSE, include=TRUE}
klippy::klippy(position = c('top', 'right'))
```


<a id='part1'></a>

# Simple map

A map in our course can be a set of polygons, lines, or points. In general, each one represents a **layer** of spatial information, and several layers together make a complex map. Let's start by loading a map of polygons, where each polygon represents a zip code area:


```{r, eval=TRUE, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE}
rm(list = ls()) # start fresh

library(sf) 
link="https://github.com/DACSS-Visual/SpatialData/raw/main/maps/wazipMap.gpkg"
zipMap=read_sf(link, layer='zips')

# preview the first rows
head(zipMap)
```

## File types

As you can see in the link above, the map is stored in a file called **wazipMap.gpkg**. The extension *.gpkg* is a common format for spatial data. Other well-known formats are **.shp** for *shapefiles* and **.geojson** (or simply **.json**) for *GeoJSON* files.

You can use a URL to read *.gpkg* or *.geojson* files directly into R, but this may not work for *.shp* files. This is because a shapefile is actually a **collection of files** rather than a single file, making it less convenient to read from a URL. If you need to share or access a shapefile remotely, it is best to convert it to *.gpkg* or *.geojson* first.

All of these formats are read using the same function: **`read_sf()`**. Note that the `layer` argument is needed when reading a *.gpkg* file (which can store multiple layers), but it is not required for *.geojson* or *.shp* files.

The object **zipMap** contains the following variables:
```{r}
names(zipMap)
```

Every spatial file must have at least two columns: one serving as an identifier (an "id"), and the mandatory **`geom`** column, which holds the geometry information (polygon, line, or point) that R uses for plotting. This file includes additional columns: the zip code of each area (*ZCTA5CE10*), and population figures for the years 2005 and 2015.

We can visualize the polygons using *ggplot2*:

```{r, warning=FALSE}

library(ggplot2)

base=ggplot(data = zipMap) # define the map
base + geom_sf(fill = 'grey80',# plot the geometry
               color = 'black',
               linewidth = 0.2)   

```

## Map projection

Since a map is a two-dimensional representation of a three-dimensional object, every map uses a **projection** — a mathematical method for translating the curved surface of the Earth onto a flat plane. The projection used depends on the geographic location of the map. The current projection for our WA state map is:



```{r}
st_crs(zipMap)$epsg
```

When the EPSG code is **4326**, the map uses geographic coordinates (longitude and latitude in degrees), which is typically the default when downloading maps from the web. This projection is **not suitable for computing distances or areas** in meaningful units (e.g., meters). For that, we need to re-project the map to a suitable coordinate reference system (CRS). You can search the web or ask an AI tool for the right EPSG code for your study area:




```{r}
# EPSG 26910 - appropriate for Washington State
zipMap_proj <- st_transform(zipMap, crs = 26910)
```

The object **zipMap_proj** is actually a new map, but now rightly projected. You can confirm the change:
```{r}
st_crs(zipMap_proj)$epsg
```

_____


[Go to table of contents.](#TOC)

________________


<a id='part2'></a>

# Choropleths


A choropleth is a colored map that represents the spatial distribution of a variable. Colors assign a visual "ranking" to each polygon relative to the others — in other words, **fill color encodes information**.

Coloring polygons is straightforward, but it can also lead to interpretation problems. For example, let's color the polygons using population data from 2005 using the [viridis colors](https://sjmgarnier.github.io/viridis/reference/scale_viridis.html): 

```{r}
base <- ggplot(data = zipMap)

base + geom_sf(aes(fill = POP2005),   # variable used to color each polygon
               color = NA) +           # no polygon borders
  scale_fill_viridis_c(direction = -1) # apply viridis color scale
```





The map above plots raw counts — the values as they are, with no transformation. Plotting raw counts generally produces a **biased visualization**: color intensity ends up visually correlated with polygon area size (larger polygons tend to have larger counts simply because they are bigger). You can read [this article](https://www.esri.com/about/newsroom/arcuser/understanding-statistical-data-for-mapping-purposes/) for further details.

Raw counts are not an intrinsic property of a polygon — if you split a polygon in two, the count changes. Therefore, we should apply a basic transformation before mapping.

---


## Densities


Using population *density* instead of raw *counts* removes the visual bias caused by polygon size, since density accounts for the area of each polygon. Let's compute the area of each polygon in km²:



```{r, message=FALSE}
library(units)

# We use 'zipMap_proj' (projected CRS) to compute area in meters, then convert to km²
# st_make_valid(): ensure all polygons have valid geometry before computation
# st_area():       compute the area of each polygon
# set_units():     convert to km²
# drop_units():    keep only the numeric value, dropping the unit label

valid_polygon <-st_make_valid(zipMap_proj)
area_m2 <-st_area(valid_polygon)
area_km2 <- set_units(area_m2, "km2")

#new column
zipMap$area_km2 <-drop_units(area_km2)

# compute population density (people per km²)
zipMap$POP2005_density <- zipMap$POP2005 / zipMap$area_km2
```


Plotting the density:
```{r}

base <- ggplot(data = zipMap)   # rebuild base plot to include the new variable

choroPop <- base + geom_sf(aes(fill = POP2005_density), color = NA)

choroPop + scale_fill_viridis_c(direction = -1)

```



## Ratios Instead of Differences

Let's now represent *change* in population between 2005 and 2015. A first instinct might be to use a **subtraction**:



```{r}
# change represented as an absolute difference (subtraction)
base + geom_sf(aes(fill = POP2015 - POP2005), color = NA) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(direction = -1, option = "B")   # "B" = "inferno" palette

```

However, absolute differences are still affected by the original size of the counts — a gain of 500 people means something very different in a zip code of 1,000 vs. one of 100,000. A **ratio**  is a better choice because it expresses change *relative* to the starting value:


```{r}

# change represented as a ratio

base + geom_sf(aes(fill = POP2015 / POP2005), color = NA) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(direction = -1, option = "B") 

```
Given the value "1" represents NO change, we could try a diverging scale:
```{r}
base + 
  geom_sf(aes(fill = POP2015 / POP2005), color = NA) +
  scale_fill_gradient2(
    low = "black",      # Color for values < 1 (Population decrease)
    mid = "white",        # Color for values = 1 (No change)
    high = "#d7191c",     # Color for values > 1 (Population increase)
    midpoint = 1,         # Your specific center point
    name = "Growth Ratio"
  )

## you may try:
  # scale_fill_distiller(
  #   palette = "RdBu",     # Red-Blue diverging palette
  #   limit = c(0.5, 1.5),  # Centering the limits helps keep 1 at the center
  #   direction = 1
  # )
```




_____


[Go to table of contents.](#TOC)

________________


<a id='part3'></a>


# Merging data into a map


## The data


Maps rarely come with rich or interesting data on their own. Most of the time, we need to bring in an external dataset and merge it into the map.


Let's work with data on contributions to candidates and political committees in Washington State. The WA [OpenData portal](https://data.wa.gov/) provides  this dataset on this [page](https://data.wa.gov/Politics/Contributions-to-Candidates-and-Political-Committe/kv7h-kjye).


```{r, eval=TRUE}
linkData='https://github.com/DACSS-Visual/SpatialData/raw/main/data/contriWA.RData'
load(url(linkData))

# NOTE: this is a regular data frame, NOT a map
head(contriWA)
```


Let's check the data types:

```{r, eval=TRUE}
str(contriWA,width = 60, strict.width = 'cut')
```

A few things to address before we can work with this data:

**1. Zip codes are stored as numeric.** We need them as character strings to match the zip code IDs in the map:


```{r}
contriWA$contributor_zip=as.character(contriWA$contributor_zip)
```


**2. The data is in long format by election year.** Let's check which years and parties are available:

```{r}
table(contriWA$election_year,contriWA$party)
```

Let's keep the Democrats and Republicans for 2012 and 2016:

```{r}
contriWA_1216_RD=contriWA[contriWA$election_year%in% c(2012,2016) & 
                          contriWA$party%in% c('DEMOCRAT','REPUBLICAN'),]
```

**3. Unused factor levels remain after subsetting.** Let's verify this and then drop them:

```{r}
# unused levels are still present after subsetting
table(contriWA_1216_RD$election_year,contriWA_1216_RD$party)
```

Here we  drop those unused levels:
```{r}
# this is a very important piece of code!!
contriWA_1216_RD$party=droplevels(contriWA_1216_RD$party)
```

## Aggregating Before Merging

To merge this data into a map of zip codes, each row in our dataset must represent **one zip code** — not one individual contribution. We therefore need to **aggregate** the data by zip code, election year, and party:


```{r, eval=TRUE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}


agg_data=aggregate(amount ~ contributor_zip + election_year + party, 
                      data = contriWA_1216_RD, 
                      FUN = function(x) c(contributors = length(x), contributions = sum(x)))
contribsWA_agg=do.call(data.frame, agg_data)
names(contribsWA_agg)[c(4,5)]=c('count_contributors','sum_contributions')
## using library(dplyr):

library(dplyr) # not really using it now.
# contribsWA_agg <- contriWA_1216_RD %>%
#   group_by(contributor_zip, election_year, party) %>%
#   summarise(
#     count_contributors  = length(amount),
#     sum_contributions = mean(amount),
#     .groups      = "drop"
#   )


# then
head(contribsWA_agg)
```


Now let's use `sum_contributions` to compute the **share_contributions**  going to each party per zip code and year:


```{r}
contribsWA_agg <- contribsWA_agg %>%
  group_by(contributor_zip, election_year) %>%
  mutate(share_contributions = sum_contributions / sum(sum_contributions) * 100) %>%
  ungroup()

contribsWA_agg

```

## Checking Coverage Before Merging

Some zip codes in the contributions data may not exist in the map, and viceversa. Let's quantify this mismatch:


```{r}
# not in zipMap 
length(setdiff(contribsWA_agg$contributor_zip,zipMap$ZCTA5CE10))
```
```{r}
# not in contribsWA_agg_W 
length(setdiff(zipMap$ZCTA5CE10,contribsWA_agg$contributor_zip))
```
Only ZIP codes present in both datasets will be retained after merging; note that several locations are lost in this process.


---

## The Merge


```{r, eval=TRUE}

# Always put the MAP on the left side of the merge.
# This ensures the result remains a spatial (sf) object.
# Reversing the order — merge(contribsWA_agg, zipMap, ...) — would lose the geometry.

contrib_zipMap <- merge(zipMap, contribsWA_agg,
                        by.x = 'ZCTA5CE10',
                        by.y = 'contributor_zip')

# now you have
head(contrib_zipMap)
```


The result, **`contrib_zipMap`**, is a new spatial object that combines the zip code geometries with the aggregated contribution data, ready for choropleth mapping.


Let's explore the contributions percapita:

```{r, eval=TRUE}

base <- ggplot() + theme_void()

base + geom_sf(data = contrib_zipMap,
               aes(fill = sum_contributions)) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(direction = -1) +       
  facet_grid(party ~ election_year) +
  labs(fill = 'sum of contributions per capita')


```

Again, remember the _sum_ is not a good choice for mapping, then try the percent (share) of contributions:

```{r}
base + geom_sf(data = contrib_zipMap,
               aes(fill = share_contributions)) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(direction = -1) +       
  facet_grid(party ~ election_year) +
  labs(fill = 'share of contributions')
```


Notice the map gives no visual reference for areas that were lost during the merge. A better approach is to add a **background layer** showing the full state boundary, so that unmatched zip codes appear as _grey_ gaps:


```{r, message=FALSE}

# load a simple WA state boundary as a background layer
wa_simple <- read_sf('https://github.com/DACSS-Visual/SpatialData/raw/refs/heads/main/maps/WA_State_Boundary.geojson')

# use wa_simple as the base layer (white fill = no data)
base <- ggplot(wa_simple) +
  geom_sf(fill = 'grey') +
  theme_void()

sharePlot=base +  geom_sf(data = contrib_zipMap,
                  aes(fill = share_contributions)) +
                  scale_fill_viridis_c(direction = -1) +       
                  facet_grid(party ~ election_year) +
                  labs(fill = 'share of contributions\n(grey: no information)')

#here
sharePlot
```

Here is some legend customization:


```{r, eval=TRUE}

sharePlot + 
      theme(legend.title    = element_text(size = 8, hjust = 0.5),
            legend.position = "bottom",
            legend.key.width = unit(1, "cm")) +
        guides(fill = guide_colourbar(title.position = "top"))
```

With a continuous color scale it is still difficult to draw clear conclusions from the map. **Discretizing** the variable into categories can make patterns easier to read and communicate.


# Discretizing

Let's organize `share_contributions` into  quartiles. We follow these steps:


1. Explore the variable to find cutpoints. 

We use `fivenum` (minimum, lower hinge, median, upper hinge, maximum) to summarize the distribution by group:


```{r}
do.call(data.frame,
        aggregate(data=contrib_zipMap,
                  share_contributions~election_year +party,
                  fivenum))
```

The same information can be seen in the boxplot:

```{r}
ggplot(contrib_zipMap) +
  aes(x = share_contributions) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  facet_grid(election_year + party ~.) 
```



2. Cut the variable:

Based on that information, let me customize the cuts:
```{r}
# vector of data breaks
qCuts=quantile(contrib_zipMap$share_contributions, probs = seq(0, 1, 0.25))
```

Now create the discrete version:
```{r}
customLabels <- c("Q1: Low", "Q2: Mid-Low", "Q3: Mid-High", "Q4: High")

contrib_zipMap$share_contributions_cat<-cut(contrib_zipMap$share_contributions, 
                                      breaks = qCuts, 
                                      labels = customLabels,include.lowest = TRUE)
#
#
# see the distribution
table(contrib_zipMap$share_contributions_cat,
      contrib_zipMap$election_year,
      contrib_zipMap$party)
```


3. Plot with a sequential palette

Choose a sequential palette from [here](https://ggplot2.tidyverse.org/reference/scale_brewer.html):


```{r}

base + geom_sf(data = contrib_zipMap,
               aes(fill = share_contributions_cat),
               color = NA) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "YlOrRd") +
  facet_grid(party ~ election_year) +
  labs(fill     = "Quartiles\n(grey: no information)",
       title    = "Share of Political Contributions by zip codes",
       subtitle = "WA State Contributions to Republicans and Democrats in 2012 & 2016") 

```

As this section shows, making a map is technically straightforward — but finding a clear, honest story to tell with it takes time and iteration.






[Go to table of contents.](#TOC)

________________


<a id='part4'></a>

# Plotting coordinates

The dataframe `contriWA_1216_RD` contains columns with geographic coordinates (longitude and latitude). We first convert it into a spatial object so it can be used with `sf`-based maps.

```{r, eval=TRUE, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE}
mapCRS <- st_crs(zipMap)   # extract the CRS from our zip code map, use it below

contriWA_1216_RD_points <- st_as_sf(contriWA_1216_RD,         # source data frame
                                     coords = c("Lon", "Lat"), # columns with coordinates
                                     remove = FALSE,  # keep the original Lon/Lat columns
                                     crs    = mapCRS)          # assign the same CRS as the map
```

The new spatial object has the same columns as before plus a `geometry` column:

```{r, eval=TRUE}
names(contriWA_1216_RD_points)
```

Unlike a regular dataframe, this spatial object is now an `sf` (simple features) object:

```{r, eval=TRUE}
class(contriWA_1216_RD_points)
```

We can confirm its geographic properties with:

```{r, eval=TRUE, warning=FALSE}
st_crs(contriWA_1216_RD_points)
```

## Simplest way with _geom_sf()_

Let's plot the points on top of the WA state zip code map using its map nature:

```{r}
# borders
base <- ggplot(wa_simple) + geom_sf(fill = 'white') +
        theme_void()

contributors <- base +
                geom_sf(data  = contriWA_1216_RD_points) +
                facet_grid(party ~ election_year) + 
                labs(title ="Location of contributors")

# see
contributors
```

This map is informative, but limited.

When many observations occupy similar locations, points overlap. This problem is known as **overplotting**.

We can partially reduce it using transparency:

```{r}
base +
  geom_sf(data  = contriWA_1216_RD_points, 
          alpha=0.1) +  # THE ALPHA
  facet_grid(party ~ election_year) 

```

Transparency helps, but not enough. We still cannot clearly identify:

- whether there is one dominant cluster or several
- how intense clusters are
- whether groups differ spatially
- whether hidden secondary clusters exist

To solve this, we use **Kernel Density Estimation (KDE)**. 

## The KDE approach

A kernel density estimate transforms raw points into a smooth surface representing local concentration.

Instead of asking:

> Where are the points?

we ask:

> Where are points concentrated?

We use `stat_density_2d()`.


Important: KDE here uses the original coordinate columns (`Lon`, `Lat`), not the `sf` geometry column.

Also note:

> These maps estimate concentration of observations (contributors), not contribution amounts.

---

## A Core Visualization Principle

When visualizing point concentration, there is always a tradeoff:

> Raw scales preserve magnitude but may hide weaker structure.  
> Transformed scales reveal weaker structure but alter intuitive magnitude interpretation.

No single map is “the truth.” Different maps answer different questions.

---

### KDE Strategy 1: Raw `level` (Linear Scale)



```{r,eval=FALSE}
stat_density_2d(aes(...,
                    fill = after_stat(level)),
                geom  = "polygon")
```



This strategy maps color directly to the kernel density estimate. Areas with twice the point density get twice the color intensity (on a continuous scale).

```{r}
base +
  stat_density_2d(
    data  = contriWA_1216_RD_points,
    aes(x = Lon, y = Lat,
        fill  = after_stat(level)),
    geom  = "polygon"
  ) +
  facet_grid(party ~ election_year) + labs(title = "kde 1")
```


> **Interpretation**

> * Color maps directly to estimated density.
> * Higher density = stronger fill.

> **Strength**

> * This is the most statistically direct representation.

> **Weakness**

> * Extremely dense regions dominate the scale. Seattle may overshadow weaker clusters.

**Use Raw Density When**

- absolute dominance matters
- comparing peak intensity matters
- data are not extremely skewed


---

### KDE Strategy 2: Log Density (`log10(level)`)



```{r,eval=FALSE}
stat_density_2d(aes(...,
                    fill = after_stat(log10(level))),
                geom  = "polygon")
```


This strategy applies a **base-10 logarithm** to density values before mapping to color. 


```{r}
base +
  stat_density_2d(data  = contriWA_1216_RD_points,
                  aes(x = Lon, y = Lat,
                      fill  = after_stat(log10(level))),
                  geom  = "polygon") +
      facet_grid(party ~ election_year) + labs(title='log KDE')
```

> **Interpretation**


> * The logarithm compresses the range of densities.

> * A 10× increase becomes one equal visual step.

> **Strength**

> * Hidden clusters become visible. Secondary cities and suburban concentrations emerge more clearly.

> **Weakness**

> * Interpretation is less intuitive. Color reflects relative ratios rather than raw magnitude.

**Use Log Density When**

- data span orders of magnitude
- one hotspot dominates
- comparing unequal groups
- exploratory analysis

---

## KDE Strategy 3: Binned Density (`as.ordered(level)`)


```{r,eval=FALSE}
stat_density_2d(aes(...,
                    fill = after_stat(as.ordered(level)), 
                    bins = 5,
                geom  = "polygon")
```

In this strategy we convert continuous density into **ordered categories** (factors), forcing, in this case, exactly 5 distinct color bands regardless of the underlying distribution shape.



```{r}
base +
  stat_density_2d(
    data = contriWA_1216_RD_points,
    aes(x = Lon, y = Lat, fill = after_stat(as.ordered(level))), # Binned
    geom = "polygon",
    bins = 5 # Force 5 distinct levels of "heat"
  ) +
  facet_grid(party ~ election_year)
```

> **Interpretation**

> * Continuous density is converted into ordered categories such as low, medium, and high.

> **Strength**

> * Easy to read and useful for presentations.

> **Weakness**

> * Boundaries are arbitrary. Fine differences disappear.

**Use Binned Density When**

- clarity matters most
- communicating to decision-makers
- building dashboards or slides
- fixed categories are useful


>Some things to try!

Try adding a viridis palette?

```{r, eval=FALSE}
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option    = "B",
                       direction = -1,
                       name      = "Density")
```


Modify `h` for example:
```{r, eval=FALSE}
stat_density_2d(..., 
                h=c(0.8,0.8))
```



## KDE Strategy 4: Normalized Density (`ndensity`)

Here we will use `after_stat(ndensity)` in fill, and use `raster` instead of `polygon.
```{r}
# By **normalizing the density within each panel** (scaling independently from 0 to 1), 
# we allow weaker secondary patterns to emerge that were previously drowned out:

base +
  stat_density_2d(
    data     = contriWA_1216_RD_points,
    aes(x    = Lon, y = Lat,
        fill = after_stat(ndensity)),
    geom     = "raster",
    contour  = FALSE,
  ) +
  facet_grid(party ~ election_year) 
```

> **What is `ndensity`?**

> Within each panel:

> - minimum approximately 0
> - maximum = 1

> * Each facet receives its own internal scale.

> **Strength**

> * Shows where each group is concentrated relative to itself. Hidden eastern Washington clusters may emerge.

> **Weakness**

> * You cannot compare magnitude across panels.

This answers:

> Where within each group?

Not:

> Which group contributes more?



NOTICE that the figure above requires some changes, let me guide you step by step to becode efficiently.

1. The basemap is not visible, let's just plot the densities:

```{r}
newbase = ggplot(contriWA_1216_RD_points) 
density = newbase + stat_density_2d(aes(x = Lon, y = Lat,
                                     fill = after_stat(ndensity)),
                                 geom     = "raster",
                                 contour  = FALSE) +
  facet_grid(party ~ election_year) 

```

2. Add better color palette
```{r}
density = density + scale_fill_viridis_c(option    = "B",
                               direction = -1,
                               name = "Normalized density") 
density
```

3. You may add the WA state border now (which used to be `base`):
```{r}
density + geom_sf(data = wa_simple, 
                  fill = NA,
                  linewidth = 0.4) 
```

## Bandwidth Matters (`h`)

KDE depends heavily on smoothing bandwidth.

```r
stat_density_2d(..., h = c(0.8, 0.8))
```

- smaller `h` = sharper, noisier local clusters
- larger `h` = smoother, broader regional structure

Bandwidth choice can strongly affect interpretation.

---

## Comparative Summary

| Question | Best Strategy |
|----------|---------------|
| Where are most contributors overall? | Raw `level` |
| Where are hidden secondary clusters? | `log10(level)` |
| Where is each party concentrated internally? | `ndensity` |
| Need a simple presentation graphic? | Binned levels |

---

## Technical Comparison

| Criterion | Raw | Log | Binned | Normalized |
|----------|-----|-----|--------|------------|
| Preserves magnitude | Yes | No | Partial | No |
| Reveals weak clusters | Low | High | Medium | Very High |
| Easy to explain | High | Medium | High | Medium |
| Cross-panel comparison | High | Medium | Medium | Low |
| Exploratory use | Good | Excellent | Fair | Excellent |

---

## Important Spatial Note

Kernel density on longitude/latitude uses degrees rather than true planar distance.

For serious spatial analysis, project coordinates first into an appropriate projected CRS.

For teaching purposes, this example remains useful.

---

## Final Synthesis

Each visualization reveals something and hides something else.

- Point maps show raw observations but suffer from overplotting.
- Raw KDE preserves magnitude but hides weaker structure.
- Log KDE reveals hidden clusters.
- Binned KDE simplifies communication.
- Normalized KDE reveals internal geography but sacrifices cross-group comparison.

Used together, these maps suggest a clear substantive pattern:

> Political contributions in Washington are strongly western and metropolitan for both parties, but Republicans retain meaningful secondary concentration in eastern Washington.

That conclusion becomes visible only by comparing multiple visualization strategies.

_____

# Interactive Maps

## Preparing Data for Web Mapping

Interactive maps created with libraries such as _leaflet_, _tmap_, or _mapview_ usually require lighter spatial data than static maps. Raw spatial objects are often too large because they contain unnecessary columns and a very high number of points defining each polygon.

For instance, in the next notebook we will use _contrib_zipMap_, which is approximately this size:

```{r}
format(object.size(contrib_zipMap), units = "auto")
```

**The Challenge**:

* The original object may be too heavy for interactive functions, making them slow or unstable.
* Polygons often contain more points than are needed for web visualization.
* The object may also include columns that are irrelevant for the map.

**The Solution:** 
We preprocess the spatial object to reduce its size before using it in interactive maps.

| Step         | Function                        | Purpose                                                 |
| ------------ | ------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------- |
| 1. Subset    | `contrib_zipMap[, keepSome]`    | Keep only the columns needed for mapping                |
| 2. Project   | `st_transform(crs = 26910)`     | Convert to a metric CRS so simplification uses meters   |
| 3. Validate  | `st_make_valid()`               | Prevent problems if any geometries are invalid          |
| 4. Simplify  | `st_simplify(dTolerance = 500)` | Reduce the number of points used to define each polygon |
| 5. Reproject | `st_transform(crs = 4326)`      | Return to geographic coordinates for web mapping        |



> The key function is `st_simplify()`, which removes points from polygon boundaries. This reduces geometric detail, but usually not in a way that is noticeable to the eye at the scale of an interactive web map. The result is a spatial object that is much more manageable in memory and faster to render.


```{r}
# subsetting
keepSome <- c("ZCTA5CE10", "election_year", "party", "share_contributions_cat")

# 1. subset
contrib_zipMap_light <- contrib_zipMap[, keepSome]

# 2. project to a metric CRS
contrib_zipMap_light <- contrib_zipMap_light %>%
  st_transform(crs = 26910)

# 3. validate geometries
contrib_zipMap_light <- contrib_zipMap_light %>%
  st_make_valid()

# 4. simplify geometry
contrib_zipMap_light <- contrib_zipMap_light %>%
  st_simplify(dTolerance = 500, preserveTopology = TRUE)

# 5. return to geographic coordinates for web mapping
contrib_zipMap_light <- contrib_zipMap_light %>%
  st_transform(crs = 4326)

```

The result will be saved as a *GeoPackage* file. Notice that, if a file with the same name already exists in the working directory, I delete it first; otherwise, the writing process may fail.

```{r}
outfile1 <- "contrib_polygons_light.gpkg"

if (file.exists(outfile1)) {
  file.remove(outfile1)
}

st_write(
  contrib_zipMap_light,
  outfile1,
  quiet = TRUE
)
```


**Also saving point data prevously created:**


```{r}
outfile2 <- "contrib_points.gpkg"

if (file.exists(outfile2)) {
  file.remove(outfile2)
}
st_write(contriWA_1216_RD_points,
  outfile2,
  quiet = TRUE
)
```


The interactive maps are here:

[map1](https://github.com/DACSS-Visual/SpatialData/blob/main/interactive.html)
[map2](https://github.com/DACSS-Visual/SpatialData/raw/main/interactive.html)
[map3](interactive.html)

[Go to table of contents.](#TOC)
